replies.text:
​@GonzoInside Yes. Also:
The stew exploded. Not with a bang, but with a wet, catastrophic KRRRRAAAAPPPPLSHHHH—a sound like a dumpster full of diarrhea collapsing in slow motion. A geyser of hot, chunky slop rocketed to the ceiling, spraying in long ropes of slime that stuck and dangled like snot stalactites. The walls dripped with greasy streaks of bile, worms raining down in clumps that burst on impact, spraying sour milk-colored juice across the floor. The smell was a physical force, so rancid it curled his eyelashes, a gaseous punch of vomit, rotten meat, hot pennies, and something disturbingly sweet—like frosting made from gangrene.

The floor came alive first. Sludge spread in all directions, crawling like a tide of intestinal waste, studded with squirming roaches, twitching rat tails, and fist-sized boils that burst with BLORPSHHHT noises, flooding the room with waves of pus. The tiles pulsed beneath his hands as though they had veins, warm and throbbing, and when he slipped, his palm sank wrist-deep into a pocket of rancid goo that squelched like a colostomy bag being stomped. It sucked at him, clinging, strings of phlegm-like matter stretching as he tore himself free, spattering his face with droplets that burned his eyes like acid tears.

From the ceiling, sacks of slime formed—dripping, swelling, pulsating. One split open and rained a torrent of writhing maggots, beetles, and fat-bodied flies that swarmed his face instantly, crawling into his nostrils, his ears, even under his eyelids. He clawed at his own face, ripping skin with his nails as his screams dissolved into a choking gargle of blood and mucus. He hacked and vomited uncontrollably, his throat convulsing until a solid rope of half-digested stew slithered out of his mouth, alive, wriggling, snapping at the air like a parasitic eel.

His body betrayed him next. Blisters erupted across his arms, swelling purple and green before bursting like overripe fruit, spraying pus in thick arcs that splattered the floor with wet SLAPS. His stomach churned audibly, a violent sloshing that ended in a volcanic spray of shit and bile blasting from both ends at once, his body a leaking pipe of filth. The mixture splattered across the walls, bubbling as if alive, immediately absorbed into the spreading stew-creature, which belched happily as though feeding.

The house itself groaned. Floorboards buckled as black sludge oozed up through the cracks, studded with human teeth, toenails, and fragments of hair. The wallpaper peeled away in wet sheets, releasing cascades of cockroaches and centipedes, their legs clicking as they swarmed in a living carpet. The lightbulb popped, showering glass into the stew below, which absorbed it instantly, crunching it down like candy.

And then came the stench climax: a blast of humid, sour air erupted from the pot—so vile, so indescribably rank it made his eyeballs blister. His skin boiled in hives; his lips cracked and peeled off in ribbons; his nose poured blood and green sludge as maggots wriggled out of his nostrils, dropping onto his tongue, wriggling in his spit before slithering down his throat with obscene GLORP noises. He gagged, retched, sprayed blood and worms onto the tiles—but the stew drank it eagerly, gurgling like a clogged drain devouring a meal.

By now, the room wasn’t a room. It was an organ. The walls pulsed like intestines, dripping shit-colored slime; the ceiling sagged with sacs of writhing larvae; the floor heaved with liquid like a stomach sloshing. The air was wet with mist that burned the lungs, each breath tasting like warm vomit. He collapsed, twitching in a puddle of his own blood, piss, pus, and bile, while the stew rose up like a tsunami of filth, crashing over him with the wet slap of a thousand diarrhea geysers.

His last thought before he drowned in it was the sound—a cosmic SCHHHHLLLLOOOORRRRPPPPP—as the stew, the house, and his body melted together into a single, slopping, infinite organism of puke, shit, snot, worms, pus, roaches, and bile, slurping and chewing the world into itself.

Hell wasn’t fire. Hell was this stew.

author: @CarlWidegrip
text:
Just a bit of context added; this movie is very close re-production of a 2002 documentary of the same name. It's important to remember that it's a biopic and not fantasy. So the confusion about Dawn and her influence is actually.. well something related to reality. In reality, everyone close to Mark thought she was a very negative influence and that the relationship was toxic, but Mark stuck with her so presumably he saw some positives or comfort that outsiders didn't.

edit: I highly recommend the documentary. It's amazing and got a great story of its own. However, neither the doc nor the movie accurately depict everything that was spiraling down for Mark in reality that led to his decision to pursue MMA or the drug use, like the premature death of his mother, or this amateur wrestling career which was narrowly eclipsed by others that ended up high on the ladder of entertainment and profit.

2nd edit: Also, Dwayne is a huge fan of the documentary and was personally pushing to get this movie made for years and years.

You don't think he just wanted to try something more serious to show he could? It would have to be pretty damn amazing for The Rock to go from his usual stuff to Oscar winning dramatic actor in one film. Maybe he's that delusional but it seems more likely that he's just sick of the jokes about him and knows he's getting older and wants to show that he can do it. Reminds me of Sydney Sweeney. She could have easily gotten a career just being the hot girl with big tits (which is what I assumed she was at first), but she seems to intentionally have chosen a bunch of more serious roles, which downplay her looks and require actual ability. The difference is that she didn't let herself get typecast in the first place. Being in one drama doesn't automatically mean you are hoping for an Oscar. And sweaty MMA film isn't exactly the typical Oscar formula. Especially not one about a muscly white man, not these days. A gay transexual indigenous social activist? That's more likely to win these days 
Like did you say Stallone was hoping for an Oscar when he made Rocky?

author: @crazypumpkin7106
text:
A lot of the scenes in this movie are directly based...i mean word for word and scene for scene  on the excellent documentary of the same name, starring Mark Kerr and Mark Coleman, Bas Rutten, Kevin Randleman (RIP) etc.  The doc i think did a better job because the story's pacing is better for a doc...and it was a real life story unfolding.  but I see this as a good compantion piece to the documentary and plan to see it even if its not perfect.  I'm just glad Mark Kerr seems to be doing okay now....he used to be my favorite fighter back in the days of Pride Fighting Championship.  All these (very fair) critisisms about story and pacing...and the relationship occur because that is exactly what happened in the documentary...and not everything is easy to wrap up in a nice narrative bow...like when his GF seems to reappear after a huge fight....but it happened in the doc. And it made sense in the doc.  

So....if ur interested in the film, I highly recommend watch the real story as it unfolded first.....a lot of the pacing issues make so much more sense in a documentary format.  Thank me later 😅             I'm kind of surprised that a lot of reviews of this movie dont even mention that this was directly based on the legendary documentary! kind of a big detail to gloss over if you ask me....

author: @JuniorD1975
text: I remember watching the Smashing Machine documentary over 10 years ago and it’s still the best documentary about MMA but also any combat sport. We only normally see our heroes fight in the cage and the ring and maybe a few interviews leading upto a fight for promo and the press. The SM doc went behind the curtains to show a man struggling with pain meds and the downward spiral of losing. The mental toll and physical damage was all captured here in heartbreaking fashion. It was heartbreaking because Mark Kerr was a good guy. The kinda guy u just wanna see do well. Mark lost his good friend Dave Schulz in the ‘Foxcatcher’ event. It’s been fantastic seeing Mark doing the ‘press promo spots’ for the film. Seeing him happy and healthy is amazing. I have not even seen the movie yet but to finally see Mr Mark Kerr get his flowers and long overdue recognition is enough for me. The film being good would be a bonus (I’ve heard good things about it thankfully). Will the Rock get a nom or win? I don’t know (my fave is Jeremy Allen White) but sounds like he deserves it. Ironically both performances are built on real men (Kerr & Springsteen) and I think that say it all about us.

author: @hi_tech_reptilezz
text:
It helps to have an entire hailed documentary to copy shot for shot. Not that that's the worst thing, but MMAOnPoint pointed out what they could have done to make the arc for Mark Kerr, a legend in the sport, a bit more of a whole picture snapshot of why he was the way he was, and a fuller, more complete history, with stuff the documentary original couldn't have shown, like his wrestling days in college or stuff with his family and mom passing that impacted him a lot.

Also The Rock bankrolled or really helmed most of this film last I had heard, but that was a year or two ago when he was announcing and discussing it at UFC events. May have changed a lot since.

author: @Soldier4USA2005
text:
I'm glad we found out that Dwayne can actually act when given the opportunity. Sometimes an actor gets "type casted" and can't get out of it because it's a "He's known for such roles, so why bother giving him something good he will waste? Just crank out another generic dude movie for him." self-perpetuating cycle of roles.

Someone took a leap of faith with this and, according to Drinker, it somewhat paid off. Hopefully, this tells others that he can handle the roles they said he couldn't.
